Based on the provided evidence, there is clear indication that the key type of interaction facilitating attachment of GIC to tooth structures arises primarily from ionic bonding rather than purely covalent ones. Specifically, the chelation reactions described extensively detail how carboxyl functional groups interact strongly with free metals (calcium notably) present in natural apatitic mineral formations constituting hard tissue substrata. While minor amounts of hydrogen-bonding might contribute secondary stabilizers augmenting overall interface cohesion, dominant force mediating intimate contact required for effective bioadhesiveness still relies fundamentally on electrostatic forces exerted via polarized charge distributions characteristic typical ionic compounds.
